title: Scratching the Surface
text:
The 30-second pitch


How can we prepare for the unknown?
Is computer vision a solved problem?
How can we save time with new tech?




You at the end of this talk




The goal of today’s talk is to help us think about preparing for a project when we don’t have all of the information that we need from the client.
For AQ Group one of the tasks that we could work on was rolled-metal defect detection. This is a problem that has been worked on for decades, and there are many classical computer vision techniques that can be used to solve it. However, there are also new vision-language models that have been shown to be very effective at a wide range of computer vision tasks.
So the question is, how can we use this new technology to save time and resources on a project like this? And how can we prepare for the unknowns that we might encounter along the way?

title: Scratching the Surface
text: What is rolled-metal?

title: Scratching the Surface
text: What are rolled-metal defects?

title: Scratching the Surface
text: This website holds the write-up for my ML portfolio project, “Scratching the Surface: Classical CV vs Vision-Language Models for steel defect detection”.

title: Scratching the Surface
text: Planning

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
What is their setup??


We just don’t know what the setup at the various AQ group factories is. Ideally, we would have a team of engineers who have set up a high quality imaging camera system that is capable of capturing high resolution images of the rolled-metal as it is being produced, as in the top left quadrant of the image. They would also have a team of data annotators who have labeled a large dataset of images with the various types of defects that can occur, as in quadrants two and three. This would allow us to train a supervised learning model to detect the defects in real-time as the metal is being produced. I think this is unlikely to be the case, because if they had gone to all of the work of putting in the cameras and annotating the data, they probably would have already trained a model to do this, and celebrated the completion of a successful project, as in the bottom right quadrant of the image. So I think it’s more likely that we will have to work with a less than ideal setup, which is something that we will have to prepare for.

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
What are rolled-metal defects?

Then make another slide with the same title and same image positions, but superimpose the classification and the annotation for the same images, to show how the defects can be subtle and how the annotations can be ambiguous. Choose some good examples from the datasets.

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
Now you guess!!
TODO: make a 2x2 grid of images from the kolektor test set, and ask the audience to guess which ones are defective and which ones are normal. Then reveal the ground truth labels and discuss how difficult it can be to classify these images, even for a human. Either look up on the web absolute position for images in quarto slides and then use the ability of quarto reveal js to progressively reveal the labels, or just make two separate slides with the same images but different annotations. The point is to show that this is a hard problem, and that even with a good camera setup and good lighting, it can be difficult to classify these images correctly.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: Yes Classical CV is a solved problem!
text:
Yes Classical CV is a solved problem!

TODO: gradually work through the various tasks of CV, and then reveal explainers/Geoffrey-Hinton.jpg and Ilya Sutskever, explainers/Geoffrey Hinton, and Alex Krizhevsky.jpeg images, and then explainers/AlexNet.png to talk about the progress in CV. You can either superimpose these images on the same slide, or make new slides below with the same title and {.uncounted} so I can move through them quickly.

Talk about the progression in Computer vision, from Mnist to ImageNet to the present day. Talk about how resnet and yolo models have very fast inference, but need the images that they are trained on to be very similar to the ones that they predict on. They are good at identifying the patterns that they have seen in the training data, but they are not good at generalizing to new domains. This is a problem for AQ group, because we don’t know what the domain of the images that we will be working with is. We might be working with images that are very different from the ones that we have seen in the training data, and this could lead to poor performance.

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
Late Fusion
Train a Large Language Model on text, then add vision as a separate input channel with a translation layer.




    
        Output Text
        
        
            
            
        

        LLM

        
            
                
                    
                    
                    
                

                
                    
                    
                

                
                    Projection
                    (Feature Vectors)
                

                
                    
                    
                

                Vision Encoder

                
                    
                    
                

                Image Input
            

            
                
                    
                    
                    
                

                
                    
                    
                

                Text Prompt
            
        
    


In short, a CLIP-based LLM is like a monolingual English speaker relying on a highly skilled translator (the adapter) to understand French (images). A natively multimodal model is truly bilingual from birth, processing and generating text, images, and audio seamlessly with the same “brain.”

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
Early Fusion
Train a Large Language Model on text and images together, with a shared internal representation.




    
        
        
            
                Text
                
                    
                    
                
            
            
            
                Image
                
                    
                    
                
            
            
            
                Video
                
                    
                    
                
            
        

        MM

        
            
                
                
            
            
                
                
            
        

        
            Any-To-Any
            Generation
        

    


In short, a CLIP-based LLM is like a monolingual English speaker relying on a highly skilled translator (the adapter) to understand French (images). A natively multimodal model is truly bilingual from birth, processing and generating text, images, and audio seamlessly with the same “brain.”

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
The domain-gap problem, visualised





Train: Severstal + NEU-DET (top). Test: KolektorSDD2 (bottom).



Same task — “is this strip defective?” — wildly different pixels.


This is the visceral “look-and-feel” of distribution shift. We didn’t change the task — we changed the camera.

Different sensors, illumination, exposure, magnification
Different defect taxonomies (scratches vs silk-spots vs welding lines)
Different aspect ratios (1600×256 vs 229×645)
Different surface treatments

A classifier trained on the top row has never seen anything that looks like the bottom row.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: What the baseline ResNet actually predicts
text:
What the baseline ResNet actually predicts

Random test images with ResNet50 prediction overlay. Green border = correct, red = wrong. The model is confidently wrong on the new domains.
Calibration is broken too — high confidence on wrong calls. This sets up Grad-CAM and the bake-off.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: Grad-CAM: peeking inside the black box
text:
Grad-CAM: peeking inside the black box





Grad-CAM heatmaps over layer4 activations. Top: Severstal+NEU samples — the model lights up on defect-correlated texture. Bottom: KolektorSDD2 — attention scatters onto seams, edges, and lighting artefacts unrelated to actual defects.



Why does the F1 collapse?
It’s not that the network “forgot how to see.” It’s that the features it learned to chase — strip-aligned texture statistics from Severstal — simply aren’t where the defects live in Kolektor.
The model is doing exactly what it was trained to do. The training distribution lied about the world.


Run with: uv run python scripts/make_gradcam_grid.py. Uses pytorch-grad-cam on layer4[-1] of resnet50_kolektor_ft_v2_best.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: Same images, frontier models, zero training
text:
Same images, frontier models, zero training





F1 and per-call cost for gpt-5.4, gemini-2.5-pro, gpt-4.1-mini on the unseen Kolektor domain.



Setup

50 images (balanced) from held-out KolektorSDD2
Identical CoT-with-JSON-schema prompt
3 providers via src/vlm_clients.py
Costs logged to results/cost_ledger.csv, capped at $50

Reading the chart

All three frontier models beat the supervised ResNet50 on Kolektor.
Gemini 2.5 Pro tops the chart at F1 0.83.
Azure mid-tier (gpt-4.1-mini) is the price–performance knee at F1 0.80 for ~10× less per call.



Source: results/vlm/flagship_summary_.json, rendered with scripts/make_flagship_bakeoff_figure.py.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: A structured-output teacher critiques a structured-output student
text:
A structured-output teacher critiques a structured-output student





F1 vs prompt-tuning round, per provider. Star = held-out F1 after final addendum.



The loop (scripts/prompt_tune_vlm.py)

Score base prompt on a 12-image calibration set
Collect every disagreement with its parsed reasoning + attributes
Feed disagreements to gpt-5.5 as a “domain prompt engineer”
Tuner emits JSON: summary, common_mistakes[], prompt_addendum
Append the addendum, re-score, repeat

The tuner output is schema-enforced — strict JSON, no free-form prose. The addendum becomes a # DOMAIN-SPECIFIC GUIDANCE block prepended to the student’s user prompt.

title: Scratching the Surface
Scratching the Surface
section: Grad-CAM points. The VLM tells you why.
text:
Grad-CAM points. The VLM tells you why.

Four images — one normal Severstal frame at the top, then three defective close-ups from KolektorSDD2 and Severstal. Middle: ResNet50 Grad-CAM heatmap (red = pixels that pushed p(defect) up). Right: live GPT-5.4 reasoning for the same image (no fine-tuning, structured-output prompt).
This is the slide that justifies pairing them. Grad-CAM = where. VLM rationale = what & why. Walk through each row: the normal Severstal is correctly called by both (the VLM volunteers “benign longitudinal texture”). The Kolektor scratch is missed by the ResNet (p=0.11, CAM points at the edges) but caught by GPT-5.4 with a clean diagnosis. The Severstal row shows both flagging it as a defect, but Grad-CAM gives you a blob and the VLM gives you “localised bright vertical mark, mechanical scratch or roller contact.” Last row is the honest failure: a partial-occlusion frame where the ResNet fires on the bright edge and the VLM declines — and tells you it’s declining because “much of the frame is dominated by a large geometric edge/occlusion.” For a process engineer triaging a flagged image, the second column is what closes the loop.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: One-off labelling + classical inference vs per-image VLM
text:
One-off labelling + classical inference vs per-image VLM





Break-even between paying a VLM per image forever vs. paying once to label N_train = 2,332 images and serving a near-free ResNet/YOLO.



Three label-cost worlds

$0.05 / label — in-house GPU-side annotators
$0.20 / label — offshore crowd workers
$0.50 / label — expert metallurgist sign-off

Break-even images processed (VLM-only vs label-once-then-classical):

$0.05/label → ~26k images
$0.20/label → ~104k images
$0.50/label → ~261k images

A line running 24/7 hits 26k images in hours. The hybrid (YOLO → escalate 30% to VLM) buys back another 3×.


Numbers from scripts/make_cost_tradeoff_figure.py, sourcing per-call VLM cost from results/vlm/flagship_summary_*.json.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: Use VLMs to teach classical CV. Don’t put them in the hot loop.
text:
Use VLMs to teach classical CV. Don’t put them in the hot loop.


What we showed

Classical CV fails under realistic domain shift — and Grad-CAM tells you why.
Frontier VLMs generalise zero-shot to unseen mills and defect taxonomies.
A prompt-tuning loop, with a structured-output teacher, moves F1 measurably on a 12-image calibration budget.
VLMs and Grad-CAM give complementary explanations — where and why.
At line-rate volumes, the break-even versus paid labelling lands in hours, not months.


The recipe for AQ Group

Day 1 — drop a VLM in shadow mode behind the existing camera. Zero labels needed.
Week 1 — harvest the VLM’s pseudo-labels. Tune the prompt against your disagreements.
Week 2 — train a small ResNet / YOLO on the pseudo-labels. Put that in the hot loop.
Ongoing — escalate the bottom-30%-confidence frames back to the VLM. Re-train weekly.

The VLM is the teacher and the safety net, not the production classifier.


This is the punchline. Everything before this slide is evidence; this slide is the recommendation.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: What are rolled-metal defects? — your turn
text:
What are rolled-metal defects? — your turn

Two Severstal hot-rolled steel-strip close-ups. One is labelled defective, one is not. Which is which?
Pair A: a normal strip and a pitting defect. Let the audience commit before clicking through. The “normal” still has texture — that’s the point: there’s no clean baseline.

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
…and here are the labels

Same images with the baseline ResNet’s prediction overlaid (green = correct, red = wrong). Even a 25M-parameter network trained on millions of images is confidently wrong on the unseen domains.
This is the audience-participation moment that motivates the whole talk: if a trained network gets this wrong with high confidence, what hope does a hand-rolled classical pipeline have?

title: Scratching the Surface
section: Yes — classical CV is a (mostly) solved problem!
text:
Yes — classical CV is a (mostly) solved problem!

The standard CV taxonomy: classification, detection, segmentation.

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
The people who solved it





Geoffrey Hinton






Ilya Sutskever, Geoffrey Hinton, Alex Krizhevsky

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
…and the network that started it

AlexNet (2012) — the architecture that ended hand-crafted feature engineering and started the deep-learning era of computer vision.
Talk about the progression in Computer vision, from Mnist to ImageNet to the present day. Talk about how resnet and yolo models have very fast inference, but need the images that they are trained on to be very similar to the ones that they predict on. They are good at identifying the patterns that they have seen in the training data, but they are not good at generalizing to new domains. This is a problem for AQ group, because we don’t know what the domain of the images that we will be working with is. We might be working with images that are very different from the ones that we have seen in the training data, and this could lead to poor performance.

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
The annotation bottleneck





Classical CV runs on labels.
Every time the camera moves, the lighting changes, or a new defect class shows up, the whole pipeline goes back to the annotator queue.
For AQ Group we don’t yet know:

which mill
which camera, which lighting rig
which defect taxonomy matters in production

So the safe bet is a system that can start without labels.


The problem is that we will have to spend a lot of time speaking to engineers and asking them to annotate data, and this is a bottleneck that we want to avoid. We want to be able to start working on the problem without having to wait for the annotations to be done, and this is where vision-language models come in.

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
Experimental setup


Train domain (what we know)

Severstal — 12,568 hot-rolled strip images, 53% defect, 1600×256 px
NEU-DET — 1,800 defect patches, 6 classes, 200×200 px

Test domain (unseen at training time)

KolektorSDD2 — 1,004 test images, binary GT mask, 229×645 px
GC10-DET — 2,294 defect images, 10 classes, Supervisely JSON


Four approaches compared (+ fine-tune ablation)



#
Approach
Training data




1
ResNet50
Severstal + NEU-DET


2
ResNet50+FT
+ Kolektor train (supervised adapt.)


3
VLM zero-shot
None (prompted only)


4
Hybrid YOLO→VLM
YOLO proposals + VLM filter


5
YOLO-bootstrap
VLM pseudo-labels only

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
Cross-domain F1 — Bake-Off




Key finding:
Classical CV (ResNet, YOLO) achieves high in-domain F1 but degrades significantly on the unseen domain.
VLM (gpt-4.1-mini, zero training) achieves F1 = 0.79 on KolektorSDD2 vs. ResNet50’s F1 = 0.16 — a 5× improvement with no task-specific fine-tuning.
The hybrid pipeline combines the speed of YOLO with the generalization of the VLM.


This is the central slide. Show the generalisation gap. Classical CV was trained on Severstal/NEU, then tested on KolektorSDD2 and GC10 — completely different cameras, mills, and defect taxonomies.

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
Supervised adaptation vs. zero-shot


The key question: if you provide ResNet50 with kolektor training labels, does it match VLM zero-shot?



Approach
Kolektor F1
GC10 F1
Labels needed




ResNet50 (baseline)
0.166
0.782
✗ kolektor


ResNet50+FT
0.562
0.647
✓ kolektor train


VLM zero-shot
0.793
0.658
✗ none



The finding: supervised adaptation closes the gap significantly (0.166 → 0.562), but the VLM zero-shot still wins.




Standard CenterCrop sees only 35% of each kolektor image


Why ResNet struggles on kolektor: these images are 2.75× taller than wide. Standard CenterCrop(224) sees only the middle 35%. v2 uses RandomResizedCrop to cover the full image. VLM processes the entire image at once — no crop needed.


ResNet50+FT v1 uses kolektor train split (2332 images: 246 defect / 2086 normal) fine-tuned at lr=5e-5 for 15 epochs starting from the Phase 2a checkpoint. Val set stays as severstal to prevent catastrophic forgetting. v2 uses RandomResizedCrop(224, scale=0.3-1.0) + composite checkpoint metric (severstal val F1 + kolektor holdout F1) / 2 to select the best epoch for both domains. v2 kolektor F1 (TTA 3-crop, τ=0.565) = 0.563 — a 32% improvement over v1. v3 warm-starts from v2 with backbone LR 10× lower to further stabilise kolektor features.
Key numbers: baseline kolektor F1 = 0.166, v1 fine-tune = 0.426 (2.6×), v2 fine-tune = 0.563 (3.5×), VLM = 0.793 (4.8×). The VLM advantage persists even with supervised adaptation — mostly due to image geometry (VLM sees the full image without crop).

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
Where classical CV breaks





ResNet50 — GC10 predictions (TP=green, FP=orange, FN=red, TN=blue)



Failure modes:

False negatives (red): subtle surface defects — oil spots, silk spots — look visually “clean” to a model trained on coarser Severstal patterns
False positives (orange): GC10’s high-contrast normal regions confuse the brightness-sensitive ResNet features
The VLM can describe why it’s uncertain — the ResNet just outputs a number

title: Scratching the Surface
section: The problem: zero labelled data at AQ
text:
The problem: zero labelled data at AQ


Scenario:
AQ Group has a camera and a conveyor belt.
They have no labelled images.
Classical CV can’t train.
YOLO can’t train.
The bootstrap experiment:
Can a frontier VLM label images well enough to train a YOLO from scratch?

Steps:

Take 500 GC10 images — labels hidden from VLM
Run gpt-4o with structured JSON prompt → pseudo-labels + bbox estimates
Train a fresh YOLO-bootstrap on pseudo-labels only
Evaluate against held-out GC10 ground truth

Cost: the entire annotation budget is the VLM API cost ($2.33 for 500 images with gpt-4o)

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
Bootstrap results





Bootstrap comparison






Approach
GC10 F1
Kolektor F1
Cost




ResNet50
0.782
0.166
human labels


ResNet50+FT
0.647
0.562
human labels + kolektor data


VLM zero-shot
0.658
0.793
per-call


YOLO-bootstrap
0.729
0.198
$2.33 API









YOLO-bootstrap trained on GC10 pseudo-labels only. Its kolektor F1 reflects zero-shot generalisation from a model never exposed to kolektor.



Annotation agreement:
gpt-4o pseudo-labels agreed with ground truth 96.8% of the time (484/500) — comparable to inter-annotator agreement for ambiguous industrial defects.


Key insight: YOLO-bootstrap’s kolektor F1 is expected to be near zero — it was trained only on GC10 pseudo-labels. Compare this with VLM zero-shot’s kolektor F1 = 0.79 with NO training at all. This makes the narrative clear: VLM pseudo-labels let you train a fast YOLO for the domain you have data for, but for a new domain, you still need the VLM’s zero-shot power or new domain-specific data.
The $2.33 annotation cost is the compelling number for GC10: we got a trainable YOLO from 500 images at $0.0047/image, vs. human annotation at $0.05–$0.20/image.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: What does this cost in production?
text:
What does this cost in production?





Approach
Cost/image
Latency
Notes




ResNet50
~$0
<5ms
GPU inference after one-time training


YOLO11s
~$0
<10ms
GPU inference


gpt-4o-mini
~$0.003
1–2s
API call per image


gpt-4o
~$0.011
2–4s
More accurate


Hybrid YOLO→VLM
~$0.001
0.5s
Only ~30% of images reach VLM




Recommendation for AQ Group:

Week 1: Deploy VLM-only on a sample (100 images/day) — immediate insight, no training data needed
Week 4: Use VLM pseudo-labels to bootstrap YOLO — cut cost 1000× at production speed
Week 8: Hybrid in production — YOLO for speed, VLM for hard cases and engineer handoff

Total investment to production: < $50 in API costs + 2 engineer-days.

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
A 3-year architectural shift at OpenAI



  
      
      
      
      
        
        
          Nov 2022
          GPT-3.5
          No Fusion
          Launch of ChatGPT. Core engine handles text tokens exclusively.
        
      

      
      
        
        
          Mar 2023
          GPT-4 (Base)
          No Fusion
          Massive logical upgrade, but architecture remains text-only.
        
      

      
      
        
        
          Sep 2023
          GPT-4V / Voice
          Late Fusion
          Stitched pipeline. External vision encoders and audio transcribers translation layers.
        
      

      
      
        
        
          May 2024
          GPT-4o
          Early Fusion
          Unified architecture. Processes text, native sight, and live audio in one neural space.
        
      

      
      
        
        
          Jul 2024
          GPT-4o mini
          Early Fusion
          Streamlined omni variant maintaining native multimodal processing.
        
      

      
      
        
        
          2025+
          GPT-5 Family
          Early Fusion
          Scales early-fusion to native video generation and seamless token blending.
        
      

  


Slide goal: Highlight the fundamental architectural paradigm shift OpenAI went through in under 3 years.

GPT-3.5 & GPT-4 base — traditional, blind LLMs. Text-in, text-out. No fusion: there were no other modalities to handle.
GPT-4V & classic Voice — late fusion. Components stitched together; information is lost (tone of voice flattened to plain text; vision pre-filtered through a separate encoder). It’s a pipeline bottleneck.
GPT-4o onwards — early fusion. No translators or adapters: images and raw audio go straight into the core network alongside text. Enables human-level latency and native audio outputs.

section: Why are VLMs better at CV tasks now?
text:
Why are VLMs better at CV tasks now?

          Stitched pipeline. External vision encoders and audio transcribers translation layers.

          Early Fusion
          Scales early-fusion to native video generation and seamless token blending.
        
      

  


Slide goal: Highlight the fundamental architectural paradigm shift OpenAI went through in under 3 years.

GPT-3.5 & GPT-4 base — traditional, blind LLMs. Text-in, text-out. No fusion: there were no other modalities to handle.
GPT-4V & classic Voice — late fusion. Components stitched together; information is lost (tone of voice flattened to plain text; vision pre-filtered through a separate encoder). It’s a pipeline bottleneck.
GPT-4o onwards — early fusion. No translators or adapters: images and raw audio go straight into the core network alongside text. Enables human-level latency and native audio outputs.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: …and here is the pixel-level ground truth
text:
…and here is the pixel-level ground truth

The bottom strip is the defect: a single pit (pink mask). The top strip is what Severstal annotators flagged as normal — texture and rolling marks, no actionable defect.
The defect is tiny — maybe 600 pixels in a 1600×256 image. Note how the “normal” still has visible texture. This is exactly why classical CV struggles: the signal-to-noise ratio at the pixel level is brutal.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: Try another pair — both have defects
text:
Try another pair — both have defects

Two more close-ups. Both strips are defective. Can you spot the marks, and would you call them the same kind of defect?
Pair B: a class-3 (“scratch”) and a class-4 (“patch”). Audience usually spots something but rarely names the class — that’s the multi-class problem on top of the binary one.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: …annotated by a metallurgist, pixel by pixel
text:
…annotated by a metallurgist, pixel by pixel

The top strip carries small “scratch” blobs (orange); the bottom strip has large “patch” regions (purple). Same dataset, same camera, four totally different defect taxonomies — and the annotator decides which one to call.
Every coloured pixel was painted by a human. ~12,500 of these images were annotated for the Kaggle Severstal challenge. That’s the bill that classical CV asks you to pay every time the camera changes.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: How fast is each model on one image?
text:
How fast is each model on one image?

Log scaleLinear scale





End-to-end inference latency per image (log scale, lower is better). Classical models are measured on an Azure NC4as_T4_v3 (T4 GPU) and an Apple-silicon CPU; VLM numbers are the mean wall-clock time per call from results/vlm/flagship_summary_*.json.







Same data on a linear axis (lower is better). The classical bars are visually invisible — that’s the point: VLMs are three to four orders of magnitude slower per call.






This is the latency reality check. A T4-served ResNet handles an image in ~6 ms — that’s ~160 images per second per GPU. The cheapest VLM in our bake-off (GPT-4.1-mini on Azure) takes ~3 seconds; Gemini-2.5-pro takes ~17. That’s three to four orders of magnitude. A 60 frames-per-second inspection line is fine for the classical model and impossible for the VLM. The point of the next slide isn’t to say VLMs are bad — it’s to say: that latency comes from somewhere, and someone has to pay for it. Flip to the linear tab to make the scale gap visceral.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: …and what does each call actually cost?
text:
…and what does each call actually cost?

Log scaleLinear scale





Same models, but USD per 1 000 images (log scale, lower is better). Classical bars are pure GPU/CPU rental on Azure ($0.526/hr for a T4); VLM bars are real API token spend pulled from results/cost_ledger.csv.







Same numbers on a linear axis (lower is better) — the classical bars collapse to a line at zero next to the VLM bars.






The latency gap shows up almost 1:1 as a cost gap. Running ResNet on a rented T4 costs fractions of a cent per thousand images — most of the bill is the VM, not the compute. Calling Gemini-2.5-pro on the same 1 000 images costs about $4.35 — roughly 5 000× more. GPT-4.1-mini sits in between at ~$0.83. So the choice isn’t “smart vs dumb model” — it’s “is the marginal accuracy from the VLM worth 4 to 5 orders of magnitude of cost?” Which sets up the break-even curve on the next slide.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: So how do the classical approaches actually score?
text:
So how do the classical approaches actually score?





Per-domain F1 across the five non-flagship approaches we tried — out-of-distribution test sets only (KolektorSDD2 + GC10-DET).






Approach
Kolektor F1
GC10 F1




ResNet50
0.16
0.78


ResNet50 + FT
0.56
0.65


YOLO11s
0.38
0.55


VLM zero-shot
0.79
0.66


YOLO-bootstrap
0.20
0.73



No single classical recipe wins both domains.

FT lifts Kolektor 3.5× but costs us on GC10 (catastrophic forgetting).
YOLO11s, trained from scratch on Severstal+NEU, lands mid-table everywhere.
A frontier VLM, with zero training on either dataset, beats every fine-tuned model on Kolektor.



This is the punchline that motivates section H. We tried the obvious things — bigger model, fine-tune on a tiny slice of the new domain, detection-head instead of classification, even bootstrap-labelling Severstal with the FT model and re-training YOLO on that. None of them generalise cleanly to both new domains. The VLM, with literally zero labelled examples from Kolektor or GC10, ties or beats every classical contender on Kolektor and is competitive on GC10. That’s not “VLMs are magic” — it’s “the domain-shift tax is so high that pretrained world knowledge is worth more than 2 332 in-domain labels.” Source: results/{resnet50,resnet50_kolektor_ft,yolo,vlm,yolo_bootstrap}/metrics.json, rendered by scripts/make_comparison_figures.py.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: A YOLO box says something a heatmap can’t
text:
A YOLO box says something a heatmap can’t

Same three images, two outputs. Middle: ResNet50 Grad-CAM — one scalar p̂(defect) plus a soft “where roughly”. Right: YOLO11s — one or more typed bounding boxes, each with its own confidence. Top two rows are in-domain Severstal; bottom row is out-of-distribution KolektorSDD2.
Qualitative companion to the scoreboard. The F1 numbers don’t tell you what each output looks like. Walk the rows:
Row 1 (Severstal, in-domain): ResNet fires on the whole bright region (p̂=1.00) — useful as a yes/no, useless if you want to know which defect. YOLO drops four tight boxes on the actual scratches with per-instance confidence. That’s a work order for an operator.
Row 2 (Severstal, in-domain): Same story — three crisp boxes vs one broad blob.
Row 3 (Kolektor, OOD): Both models break, but differently. ResNet says “no defect” (p̂=0.11) and the CAM is on irrelevant texture. YOLO does fire — two low-confidence boxes — but they’re at the top, not on the actual scratch in the middle.
Neither is great on the OOD row. The point of the slide is different output shape, same domain-gap problem — bringing more classical machinery to bear doesn’t close the gap, it just changes what “failure” looks like. Time to try something different.

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
Datasets at a glance











Dataset
Role
Train
Test
Image size
Native taxonomy




Severstal
Train
10,053
1,259
1600 × 256
4 classes (pit, scratch, patch, ridge)


NEU-DET
Train
1,700
100
200 × 200
6 classes (crazing, inclusion, …)


KolektorSDD2
Holdout
2,332
1,004
230 × 630–1240
1 binary mask



All labels collapsed to a single binary defect / no-defect target. The two TRAIN POOL datasets are what ResNet50 and YOLO11s see during training. KolektorSDD2 is the new mill — different camera, different lighting, different defect vocabulary — that the classical models have to generalise to.

The point of the table is to be concrete about what the models do and do not see. Severstal + NEU-DET ≈ 11 700 labelled images of two specific imaging setups. KolektorSDD2 is reserved as the held-out domain: same task (“is there a defect?”), totally different pixels. NEU-DET ships all-defect (no normals) — that’s what a lot of real factory data looks like before anyone has bothered to annotate a clean baseline.

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
More labels won’t save us





Held-out F1 for every classical recipe we tried.



No classical recipe gets us there.
Fine-tuning on Kolektor helps — F1 climbs from 0.16 to 0.56 — but still leaves us far short of production-ready.
~11 700 labels in, and the ceiling is a passable model on one mill.


This slide earns the pivot to VLMs. We tried the obvious classical things — bigger model, fine-tune on a slice of the new domain, detection head (YOLO11s), self-labelling Severstal and re-training YOLO on those pseudo-labels. None of them clear the bar on KolektorSDD2. Fine-tuning on Kolektor lifts F1 3.5× but plateaus around 0.56. Numbers (held-out F1 on Kolektor):

ResNet50: 0.16
ResNet50+FT: 0.56
YOLO11s: 0.38
YOLO-bootstrap: 0.20

The figure is deliberately all classical — the VLM hasn’t entered the story yet. Audience should leave this slide asking “what else could possibly help?”. Source: results/{resnet50,resnet50_kolektor_ft,yolo,yolo_bootstrap}/metrics.json, rendered by scripts/make_comparison_figures.py (f1_comparison_classical.png).

title: Scratching the Surface
section: We make some assumptions about the project spec
text:
We make some assumptions about the project spec
We will treat the problem as if they have

no labelled data,
but want us to as quickly as possible provide them with a system that can identify defects on the production line.

Then in this demo, I choose

two datasets that have some similarities to what we might expect from AQ Group as training data
and use another dataset as a holdout set to test how well our models generalise to new domains.

So how do we go about this??

title: Scratching the Surface
text:
Two classical workhorses





ResNet50 — image-level classifier. One number out: \(\hat{p}(\text{defect})\).






YOLOv8 / YOLO11s — object detector. Typed bounding boxes with per-box confidence.




Two architectures we’ll lean on. ResNet50 is the canonical image classifier — convolutional backbone, pooled features, one sigmoid output. YOLO is the canonical real-time detector — same kind of backbone, but the head predicts a grid of bounding boxes with class scores. ResNet gives us “is this strip defective?”. YOLO gives us “and here’s where, with a box per instance”. Both are pre-trained on ImageNet / COCO and then fine-tuned on Severstal + NEU-DET.

title: Scratching the Surface
text: Early Fusion shared embedding space

title: Scratching the Surface
section: Best VLM vs every classical recipe — KolektorSDD2 F1
text:
Best VLM vs every classical recipe — KolektorSDD2 F1





Held-out F1 on KolektorSDD2: every classical recipe vs the best frontier VLM (zero-shot).



The headline.

Best classical recipe (ResNet50 + Kolektor FT): 0.56
Best zero-shot VLM (Gemini 2.5 Pro): 0.83

No labels on the new domain, no fine-tuning, no schema changes — just an API call with a prompt.


This is the punch-line of the bake-off. We poured ~11 700 labels and a fine-tune into the classical side; the VLM saw nothing of Kolektor before inference. Same metric, same images, very different ceiling. Rendered by scripts/make_comparison_figures.py (f1_kolektor_vlm_vs_classical.png), VLM number pulled from results/vlm/flagship_summary_*.json.

Same metric, same images, very different ceiling. Rendered by scripts/make_comparison_figures.py (f1_kolektor_vlm_vs_classical.png), VLM number pulled from results/vlm/flagship_summary_*.json.

title: Scratching the Surface
section: Does the student ever catch the teacher?
text:
Does the student ever catch the teacher?





ResNet50 trained on VLM pseudo-labels at increasing N (3 seeds per N). Dashed line = GPT-5.4 zero-shot F1 = 0.79.



Setup (scripts/labelling_curve.py)

Pool: Kolektor train (~2 300 images, ~10 % defective)
Labels: VLM pseudo-labels with measured noise (TPR=0.68, TNR=0.96)
3 seeds per N — no class balancing
6 epochs, AdamW, ResNet50

Reading the chart

At N ≤ 1 600, the student has not caught the teacher (F1 peaks ~0.37 vs 0.79).
The gap signals that more labels and/or a cleaner label budget would be needed.
The dollar axis makes the trade-off concrete: $10.74 of labelling still falls short.



The student (ResNet50) never catches the teacher (GPT-5.4 zero-shot) within the 1 600-image budget tested. This is an honest result — the teacher is very good and the labels are noisy. The practical implication is that you need either more images, better labels, or a fine-tuned VLM starting point before the classical model matches.
